questions.question: Explain the difference between SIMD and MIMD architectures with examples. (3 marks)
keyPoints: SIMD: Single Instruction, Multiple Data -- one instruction operates on multiple data elements in lockstep. Example: GPU shader cores, AVX vector instructions.
MIMD: Multiple Instruction, Multiple Data -- each processor independently executes different instructions on different data. Example: multi-core CPUs, clusters.
SIMD has centralized synchronous control (data parallelism only); MIMD has distributed asynchronous control (supports both data and task parallelism).

questions.question: What is memory contention in shared-memory MIMD systems and how can it be mitigated? (3 marks)
keyPoints: Memory contention occurs when multiple processors compete to access the same memory location or bus simultaneously, causing serialization and performance degradation.
Mitigation strategies: use NUMA architecture (local memory is faster), use private caches to reduce memory traffic, minimize shared data access, use lock-free algorithms.
Cache coherence protocols help by keeping data in local caches, reducing main memory accesses.

questions.question: Compare and contrast shared memory and distributed memory MIMD systems, discussing their advantages, disadvantages, and typical use cases. (8 marks)
keyPoints: Memory architecture: shared memory has a global address space; distributed memory has private per-node memory.
Communication: shared memory uses read/write to common variables; distributed memory uses explicit message passing (MPI).
Scalability: shared memory limited to hundreds of cores (bus bottleneck, cache coherence); distributed memory scales to thousands+ of cores.
Programming: shared memory is easier (OpenMP, implicit sharing); distributed memory is harder (MPI, explicit communication).
Cache coherence: required in shared memory (MESI protocol overhead); not needed in distributed memory.
Use cases: shared memory for workstations, servers (multi-core); distributed memory for supercomputers, clusters, cloud.
Modern systems are hybrid: clusters of multi-core nodes using MPI + OpenMP.

questions.question: Explain the MESI cache coherence protocol. Describe the four states and give an example of a state transition sequence when two processors access the same memory location. (5 marks)
keyPoints: Modified (M): cache has only copy, data differs from main memory (dirty).
Exclusive (E): cache has only copy, data matches main memory (clean).
Shared (S): multiple caches hold the line, all match main memory (clean, shared).
Invalid (I): cache line is not valid (stale or empty).

Example: P0 reads address X (no one else has it) -> line goes to E state. P1 reads X -> P0's line transitions to S, P1 gets S. P0 writes to X -> P0's line goes to M, P1's line becomes I (invalidated).
